ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKM covers raw beef meat broadly; no fillet-specific FoodEx2 code identified. Lean trimmed fillet is a subset of this category. 'closest_parent' is used as the match type.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw lean beef fillet is approximately 100 g, which yields roughly 70–80 g after cooking due to moisture loss.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, tenderloin, separable lean only, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 23190); cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy 136 kcal, protein 22.2 g, fat 5.2 g, carbohydrate 0 g, sugar 0 g, sodium 57 mg, potassium 380 mg, calcium 6 mg, iron 2.2 mg, cholesterol 58 mg) are sourced from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for lean raw beef fillet, which is an approved primary source under Project Veritas v2. Fatty acid fractions (saturated fat 1.9 g, monounsaturated fat 2.1 g, polyunsaturated fat 0.2 g, trans fat 0.1 g) are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for Beef, tenderloin, separable lean only, raw (FDC ID 174036), which is an approved secondary global source. Both sources are on the approved source list. This is an 'adapted' match combining two approved sources to provide a complete nutritional profile.

gi_source.title: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates; GI concept not applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: Beef fillet, raw, lean contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g as confirmed by HPB Singapore Food Composition Database. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. Per Project Veritas v2 critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods: glycemic_index is set to null (not 0), gi_category is set to 'not_applicable', and glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 by definition. No GI database lookup is required or appropriate for this food type. University of Sydney GI Database does not assign GI values to zero-carbohydrate animal protein foods.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (57 mg/100 g) and high potassium (380 mg/100 g) yield a favourable Na:K ratio consistent with DASH dietary principles.
hypertension.guidance: Lean raw beef fillet is suitable for hypertensive individuals when prepared without added salt or high-sodium marinades; limit portion to 90–120 g cooked and season with herbs and spices instead of salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar content means no glycaemic impact; high protein (22.2 g/100 g) supports satiety and blood glucose stability.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lean beef fillet is appropriate for people with Type 2 Diabetes as a high-protein, zero-carbohydrate protein source; pair with non-starchy vegetables and limit portion to 90–120 g cooked to manage total energy intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content (~1.9 g/100 g) and cholesterol (58 mg/100 g) are present but relatively low for a lean trimmed cut; trans fat is approximately 0.1 g/100 g (naturally occurring ruminant trans fat, primarily vaccenic acid).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lean beef fillet may be included in a heart-healthy diet in moderate portions (90–120 g cooked, 1–2 times per week) when prepared without added saturated fats; individuals with established CVD should prioritise oily fish and plant proteins as primary protein sources.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat contains arachidonic acid, a precursor to pro-inflammatory eicosanoids relevant to RA disease activity; no anti-inflammatory omega-3, polyphenols, or dietary fibre are present to offset this.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit red meat including lean beef fillet to no more than 1–2 servings per week and consider substituting with oily fish such as salmon or sardines to obtain anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef fillet, raw and lean, is the uncooked tenderloin muscle of beef cattle with all separable external and internal fat removed. It is one of the most tender cuts of beef due to the low workload of the psoas major muscle, which runs along the spine. The raw meat displays a deep red colour and fine-grained texture with minimal visible marbling in its lean-trimmed form. Per 100 g, it provides 136 kcal, 22.2 g protein, 5.2 g total fat (approximately 1.9 g saturated), 0 g carbohydrate, 0 g sugar, 57 mg sodium, 380 mg potassium, 6 mg calcium, 2.2 mg iron, and 58 mg cholesterol. The high protein content supports muscle maintenance and satiety. The very low sodium and high potassium content make it favourable for blood pressure management under DASH dietary guidelines. The absence of carbohydrate means it has no glycaemic impact, making it suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes as a protein source. The moderate saturated fat and cholesterol content warrant portion control for individuals with cardiovascular disease. As a red meat, it contains arachidonic acid and should be limited by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is suitable for halal and keto dietary patterns. It is used globally in Western, European, and fine-dining contexts as steak, carpaccio, beef tartare, and as a component of beef Wellington.
common_user_queries: How many calories in raw lean beef fillet?
Is beef tenderloin good for diabetes?
Is beef fillet high in protein?
Can I eat beef fillet if I have high blood pressure?
How much iron is in beef fillet?
Is beef fillet low in fat?
What is the difference between beef fillet and tenderloin?

confidence_reason: Core macros and micronutrients sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database at confidence 0.95 (exact match for lean raw beef fillet). Fatty acid fractions supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 174036 (Beef, tenderloin, separable lean only, raw), which is an exact match for this food category. Both are approved primary/secondary sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Food Composition Database — beef fillet is a pure muscle meat with no carbohydrate content (naturally zero). Sugar_g is naturally zero as no sugars are present in raw lean muscle meat. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is an animal muscle food containing no plant fibre. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0 g/100 g); gi_category set to 'not_applicable' and glycemic_index set to null per Project Veritas v2 critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods. Saturated_fat_g (1.9 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (2.1 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.2 g), and trans_fat_g (0.1 g) are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 174036 (Beef, tenderloin, separable lean only, raw) as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions separately. The trans fat present is naturally occurring ruminant trans fat (primarily vaccenic acid), not industrially produced trans fat. Fields set to -1 (data not available): vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed vitamin and mineral panel beyond those reported by HPB not incorporated to maintain source consistency with the HPB primary record; these values were not included to avoid mixing confidence levels across a larger number of fields. Halal suitability is conditional on slaughter method compliance with halal standards. FoodEx2 code A0CKM is a broad match for raw beef meat; 'closest_parent' match type is used as no fillet-specific FoodEx2 code was identified in the FoodEx2 catalogue as of 2026. This is a generic Western ingredient; no mandatory non-English local-language names are required under G6 multilingual rules.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is the precise canonical English name 'Beef fillet, raw, lean'. This is a generic Western ingredient with no mandatory local-language identity requirement under G6 multilingual rules. Identity is unambiguous.

Core macros sourced from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (approved primary source). Fatty acid fractions from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 174036 (approved secondary source). nutrient_source.title, organization, url, and source_match_type all correctly reflect the combined approved sources. carbohydrate_g zero is justified as naturally zero for raw lean muscle meat and documented in notes.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.0 — naturally zero, documented), sodium_mg (57.0), saturated_fat_g (1.9 — from USDA SR Legacy, approved source), sugar_g (0.0 — naturally zero, documented). All four required secondary fields present as numeric values.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index set to null (not 0) as food contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g, satisfying the critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods. gi_category set to 'not_applicable'. glycemic_load_per_serving set to 0.0. gi_source documents the not-applicable rationale with explicit reference to University of Sydney GI Database not assigning GI values to zero-carbohydrate animal protein foods. This satisfies the q4 low/zero-carbohydrate food rule.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI provided (FOODON_03311434, beef tenderloin raw — exact match). FoodEx2 code A0CKM (raw beef meat — 'closest_parent' match type, gap noted). SNOMED CT code 226934003 (Beef fillet substance — exact match). At least one defensible ontology anchor present. No codes invented.

Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. Rheumatoid arthritis included as additional condition.

veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Beef fillet, raw, lean (F06030010) passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8 on the Q1–Q8 scorecard after three iterations. The primary fixes in iteration 3 were: (1) nutrient_source now correctly cites USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 174036 with a direct URL as the approved secondary source for fatty acid fractions, resolving G2 and Q2 failures; (2) gi_source now explicitly references University of Sydney GI Database policy for zero-carbohydrate foods, resolving Q4 failure; (3) glycemic_index confirmed as null per the critical rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods. All other criteria remain satisfied from iteration 2.
